concepts.title: Stress
concepts.conceptBody:
Stress 

When an external force is applied to a body then at each cross section of the body an internal restoring force is developed which tends to restore the body to its original state. The internal restoring force per unit area of cross section of the deformed body is called stress. It is usually denoted by $\sigma $ (sigma).

Thus, $\text{ Stress }\left( \sigma \right)=\frac{\text{ Restoring }\text{ force }}{\text{ Area }}$

Depending upon the way the deforming forces are applied to a body, there are three types of stress: longitudinal stress, shearing stress and volume stress.

concepts.title: Longitudinal and Shearing Stress
concepts.conceptBody:
Longitudinal and Shearing Stress 

The body of figure is in static equilibrium under an arbitrary set of external forces. In Fig. (b), we see the same body with an imaginary sectional cut at CC'. Since each of the two individual parts of the body is also in static equilibrium, both internal forces and internal torques are developed at the cross section. Those on the right portion are due to the left portion and vice-versa.

 

On the left portion, the normal and tangential components of the internal forces are ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{n}}}$ and ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{t}}}$ respectively, and the net internal torque is $\vec{\tau }$. From Newton's third law, the right portion is subjected at this same cross section to force components $-{{\vec{F}}_{\text{n}}}$ and $-{{\vec{F}}_{\text{t}}}$ and the torque $-\vec{\tau }$. We define the normal stress or longitudinal stress over the area as,

${{\sigma }_{\text{n}}}=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{\text{n}}}}{\text{A}}$

and the tangential stress or shearing stress over the area as,

${{\sigma }_{\text{t}}}=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{\text{t}}}}{\text{A}}$

Here, A is the cross-section area of the body at $\text{C}{{\text{C}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$. The longitudinal stress can be two types. The two parts of the body on two sides of a cross section may pull each other. The longitudinal stress is then called the tensile stress. This is the case when a rod or a wire is stretched by equal and opposite forces. In case of tensile stress in a wire or a rod, the force ${{\text{F}}_{\text{n}}}$ is just the tension.

If the rod is pushed at the two ends with equal and opposite forces, it will be under compression. Taking any cross-section of the rod the two parts on the two sides push each other. The longitudinal stress in this case is called the compressive stress.

concepts.title: Volume Stress
concepts.conceptBody:
Volume Stress

 

When a body is acted upon by forces in such a manner that,

(i) the force at any point is normal to the surface.

(ii) the magnitude of the force on any small area is proportional to the area.

The force per unit area is then called the volume stress, i.e.,

${{\sigma }_{\text{v}}}=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{A}}$

which is same as the pressure. This is the case when a body is immersed in a liquid.

concepts.title: Strain
concepts.conceptBody:
Strain 

Consider a body of square cross section ABCD. Four forces of equal magnitude $\text{F}$ are applied as shown in figure. Net resultant force and net torque is zero. Hence, the body is in translational as well as rotational equilibrium. Because of the forces the shape of the cross section changes from a square to a parallelogram.

We define the shearing strain as the displacement of a layer divided by its distance from the fixed layer. Thus, shearing strain

$\varepsilon =\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}}{\text{x}}$

concepts.title: Types of Strain
concepts.conceptBody:
Types of strain 

  1. longitudinal strain $=\frac{\text{ change }\text{ in }\text{ length }}{\text{ Original }\text{ length }}\frac{\left( \Delta\text{ l} \right)}{\text{l}}$
  2. Shearing strain $=\theta =\frac{\Delta l}{l}=\frac{\begin{matrix} \text{ displacement }\text{ of }\text{ a }\text{ surface } \\ \text{ under }\text{ a }\text{ tangential }\text{ force } \\ \end{matrix}}{\begin{align} & \text{ perpendicular }\text{ distance }\text{ of }\text{ the }\text{ displaced} \\ & \text{ surface from the fixed surface} \\ \end{align}}$
  3. volumetric strain $=\frac{\text{ change }\text{ in }\text{ volume }}{\text{ original }\text{ volume }}=\frac{\left( \Delta \text{ v} \right)}{\text{v}}$

concepts.title: Hooke's law
concepts.conceptBody:
Hooke's law

If the deforming forces are within a limit (known as elastic limit), the stress created in the body is proportional to the resulting strain.

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ stress $\propto $ strain.

The ratio $\frac{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ stress }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ strain }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}$ is known as modulus of Elasticity.

concepts.title: Young's Modulus
concepts.conceptBody:
Young's Modulus

 

If a body is deformed by applying forces along one dimension only, then within limits of proportionality, the ratio of linear stress either compressive or tensile to the longitudinal strain is called the Young's modulus of the material of the body and is represented by the letter Y,

$\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., } \text{ Y}=\frac{\text{ Linear } \text{ stress } \text{ }}{\text{ Longitudinal } \text{ strain } \text{ }}$

Thus, if a rod or wire of length $\text{L}$ and cross-sectional area A under the action of a stretching force $\text{F}$ applied normally to its face suffers a change $\text{ }\Delta\text{ L}$ in its length, then in equilibrium :

$\text{ Tensile } \text{ stress } \text{ }=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{\bot }}}{\text{ area } \text{ }}=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{A}}$

and Longitudinal strain $=\frac{\text{ Change } \text{ in } \text{ length } \text{ }}{\text{ Original } \text{ length } \text{ }}=\frac{\text{ }\Delta\text{ L}}{\text{L}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \therefore \text{ Y} &  =\frac{\text{ Tensile } \text{ stress } \text{ }}{\text{ Longitudinal } \text{ strain } \text{ }} \\ {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

$=\frac{\text{F}/\text{A}}{\text{ }\Delta\text{ L}/\text{L}}=\frac{\text{FL}}{\text{A }\Delta\text{ L}}$ … (2)

concepts.title: Thermal Stress Overview
concepts.conceptBody:
THERMAL STRESSES AND STRAINS 

Whenever there is some increase or decrease in the temperature of the body, it causes the body to expand or contract. If the body is allowed to expand or contract freely, with the rise or fall of the temperature, no stresses are induced in the body. But if the deformation of the body is prevented, some stresses are induced in the body. Such stresses are called thermal stresses or temperature stresses. The corresponding strains are called thermal strains or temperature strains. Consider a rod $\text{AB}$ fixed at two supports as shown in figure.

Let $1=$ length of $\text{rod}$

$\text{A}=$ area of cross section of the rod

$\text{Y}=$ Young's modulus of elasticity of the rod

and $\alpha =$ thermal coefficient of linear expansion of the rod

Let the temperature of the rod is increased by an amount t. The length of the rod would had increased by an amount $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ l}$, if it were not fixed at two supports. Here

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ l}=\text{l}\alpha \text{t}$

But since the rod is fixed at the supports a compressive strain will be produced in the rod. Because at the increased temperature, the natural length of the rod is $1+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }l$, while being fixed at two supports its actual length is 1 . Hence thermal strain

$\varepsilon =\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ l}}{\text{l}}=\frac{\text{l}\alpha \text{t}}{\text{l}}=\alpha \text{t}$

Or $\varepsilon $ $~=\alpha \text{t}$

Therefore, thermal stress $\sigma $ $~=\text{Y}\varepsilon $ $\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ stress }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{Y }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ strain }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

Or $\sigma =\text{Y}\alpha \text{t}$

or force on the supports,

$\text{F}=\sigma \text{A}=\text{YA}\alpha \text{t}$

This force $\text{F}$ is in the direction shown below :
